report_name: Iranian APT Screening Serpens Unleashes New RATs in Espionage Campaign Against US, Israel, and UAE

threat_actors.name: Screening Serpens
threat_actors.description: An Iran-nexus Advanced Persistent Threat (APT) group that conducted cyberespionage campaigns between February and April 2026 against technology, aerospace, and defense sectors. The group is noted for using AppDomainManager hijacking to disable security controls and deploying new RAT families MiniUpdate and MiniJunk V2.
threat_actors.goals: Cyberespionage to acquire sensitive intellectual property, defense secrets, and other confidential information.

attack_patterns.name: Spearphishing Attachment
attack_patterns.description: The infection chain begins with a malicious archive file (e.g., `Hiring Portal.zip`) delivered via targeted social engineering lures related to job recruitment.

attack_patterns.name: Malicious File
attack_patterns.description: The threat actor uses a malicious archive file containing a payload to initiate the infection chain.

attack_patterns.name: Registry Run Keys / Startup Folder
attack_patterns.description: The MiniUpdate and MiniJunk V2 malware families establish persistence on compromised systems using this technique.

attack_patterns.name: DLL Sideloading
attack_patterns.description: The actor uses DLL sideloading to execute the payloads of the MiniUpdate and MiniJunk V2 malware.

attack_patterns.name: Deobfuscate/Decode Files or Information
attack_patterns.description: This technique was observed as part of the actor's TTPs, likely used to unpack or reveal malicious payloads during execution.

attack_patterns.name: Disable or Modify Tools
attack_patterns.description: The actor uses AppDomainManager hijacking to modify legitimate .NET application configurations, which forces the loading of a malicious assembly to programmatically disable security features like AMSI.

attack_patterns.name: Web Protocols
attack_patterns.description: The MiniUpdate and MiniJunk V2 malware families communicate with their command and control servers over standard web protocols.

attack_patterns.name: Exfiltration Over C2 Channel
attack_patterns.description: Later variants of the MiniUpdate malware include the capability to exfiltrate files in chunks over the existing C2 channel.

attack_patterns.name: MSBuild
attack_patterns.description: The actor may abuse MSBuild.exe to execute malicious code, which can be hunted for by monitoring for suspicious child processes or outbound network connections.

attack_patterns.name: System Binary Proxy Execution: InstallUtil
attack_patterns.description: The actor may abuse InstallUtil.exe to execute malicious code, which can be detected by monitoring for it spawning unusual child processes like cmd.exe or powershell.exe.

malware.name: MiniUpdate
malware.description: A new RAT family deployed by Screening Serpens, named after the internal filename 'UpdateChecker.dll'. It was used against targets in the U.S., Israel, and the UAE, with later versions capable of exfiltrating files in chunks.

malware.name: MiniJunk V2
malware.description: A new RAT family used by Screening Serpens that builds on their previous tooling. It is delivered via spear phishing and uses DLL sideloading for execution.

tools.name: MSBuild
tools.description: Legitimate Microsoft Build Engine utility mentioned as a potentially abused process for executing malicious code. Security teams are advised to monitor it for spawning suspicious child processes or making outbound network connections.

tools.name: InstallUtil
tools.description: Legitimate .NET Framework utility that can be abused by attackers to execute malicious code. The report suggests monitoring for this process spawning unusual child processes.

identities.name: Technology Sector
identities.description: A primary target sector for Screening Serpens' 2026 cyberespionage campaigns.

identities.name: Aerospace Sector
identities.description: A primary target sector for Screening Serpens' 2026 cyberespionage campaigns.

identities.name: Defense Sector
identities.description: A primary target sector for Screening Serpens' 2026 cyberespionage campaigns.

infrastructure.name: Screening Serpens Azure C2 Infrastructure
infrastructure.description: A set of unique, target-specific domains hosted on Microsoft Azure used for command and control to enhance operational resilience and avoid cross-contamination between campaigns.

indicators.name: Screening Serpens Malware Artifacts
indicators.x_netsecio_sigma_rule:
title: Screening Serpens Malware Artifacts
id: 7f5b1a3c-8d9e-4f7a-b1c2-9e8d7f6a5b4d
status: experimental
description: Detects the presence of files associated with Screening Serpens campaigns, such as the MiniUpdate RAT and initial delivery archives.
references:
  - https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/tracking-iran-apt-screening-serpens/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-05-22
tags:
  - attack.t1204.002
level: high
logsource:
  category: file_event
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    TargetFilename|endswith:
      - '\Hiring Portal.zip'
      - '\UpdateChecker.dll'
  condition: selection
indicators.description: Detects known filenames used in Screening Serpens campaigns, including the initial spearphishing attachment and an internal name for the MiniUpdate malware.

indicators.name: .NET AppDomainManager Hijacking via Command Line
indicators.pattern:
title: .NET AppDomainManager Hijacking via Command Line
id: 1a2b3c4d-5e6f-4a7b-8c9d-0e1f2a3b4c5d
status: experimental
description: Detects command line activity that attempts to add the 'appDomainManagerAssembly' element to a .NET configuration file. This technique is used by groups like Screening Serpens to hijack application startup and disable security features.
references:
  - https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/tracking-iran-apt-screening-serpens/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-05-22
tags:
  - attack.t1562.001
  - attack.defense_evasion
level: high
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    CommandLine|contains|all:
      - 'appDomainManagerAssembly'
      - '.exe.config'
  condition: selection
indicators.description: Detects attempts to perform AppDomainManager hijacking by writing the required XML tag to a .NET application's .config file via the command line.

indicators.name: Suspicious MSBuild Network Connection
indicators.pattern:
title: Suspicious MSBuild Network Connection
id: 6f7a8b9c-0d1e-4f2a-b3c4-d5e6f7a8b9c0
status: experimental
description: Detects MSBuild.exe making outbound network connections. This can be an indicator of abuse by threat actors like Screening Serpens for malicious code execution and C2 communication.
references:
  - https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/tracking-iran-apt-screening-serpens/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-05-22
tags:
  - attack.t1127.001
  - attack.defense_evasion
level: medium
logsource:
  category: network_connection
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    Image|endswith: '\MSBuild.exe'
    Initiated: true
  filter_local:
    DestinationIp:
      - '127.0.0.1'
      - '::1'
  condition: selection and not filter_local
indicators.description: Detects the legitimate Microsoft Build Engine, MSBuild.exe, making outbound network connections, which is anomalous and could indicate its abuse for C2 communications or tool transfer.
